short: A word like mio, tuo, suo that marks ownership before a noun, agreeing with the thing owned and normally taking the definite article (il mio libro, la mia casa).
long: Italian possessive adjectives (mio, tuo, suo, nostro, vostro, loro) agree in gender and number with the noun owned, not with the owner, and normally take the definite article: il mio, la mia, i miei, le mie. Loro is invariable. Singular, unmodified family nouns drop the article (mia madre), but it returns in the plural, under modification, with affectionate forms, and always with loro.

short: The possessive used on its own, standing in for a noun already understood, and keeping its article (il mio, la tua).
long: When the noun is dropped, the possessive stands alone as a pronoun and keeps the definite article: 'La mia macchina è vecchia, la tua è nuova' (yours). It still agrees in gender and number with the understood noun. After essere the article may drop in the pure predicate of ownership (è mio, it's mine), which is a distinct construction from the article-bearing pronoun.

short: The rule that a singular, unmodified family noun drops the article after a possessive: mia madre, not la mia madre.
long: Singular, unmodified nouns for close relatives (madre, padre, fratello, sorella, zio, zia, moglie, marito, figlio, figlia) take no article after mio, tuo, suo, nostro or vostro: mia madre. The article returns in the plural (i miei fratelli), when the noun is modified (la mia sorella maggiore), when it is altered or affectionate (la mia mamma, il mio fratellino), and always with loro (il loro padre).
